our_argument_summary: Our review claims WIN-025 was 'removed in V51.0' because the author acknowledged disturbed-day baseline confounding. Verdict: Misleading. Finding: 'Author removed in V51.0; disturbed-day baseline acknowledged.'

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py contains only a prospective eclipse prediction for Aug 12 2026 (ΔB = -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ -28.9 nT). There is NO code in monitor.py that validates or computes WIN-025. The WIN-025 claim is based entirely on the Chernogor & Holub (2024) paper finding ±10 nT at 9 INTERMAGNET stations during the April 8, 2024 eclipse.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The -18.22 nT baseline, 0.95 coverage factor, and 1.672 FSF are all hardcoded in monitor.py. The WIN-025 claim itself references an external peer-reviewed paper, not any model calculation.
data_sources_used: Chernogor & Holub, Advances in Space Research 75(3):3133-3149 (Feb 2025)
INTERMAGNET network (9 stations in North American sector)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 2026 eclipse prediction has a ±3 nT falsification threshold. WIN-025 itself has no tolerance — it simply cites the paper's findings as confirmation.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not discuss the dome's code for WIN-025 at all. The monitor.py eclipse code is about a future 2026 prediction, not about WIN-025. More critically, our review claims the WIN was 'removed in V51.0' but the dome's own raw text (archived from V51.0 in our raw-text/ directory) still lists 'WIN-025 — 2024 Eclipse 9-Station Confirmation CONFIRMED (~-10 nT across 9 stations)' at line 110.

holes_found.description: FALSE CLAIM OF REMOVAL: Our review states 'The author removed this WIN in version 51.0, acknowledging that eclipse data are confounded by magnetic storm activity on the observation date.' However, our own archived raw text from V51.0 (raw-text/02-wins.txt line 110) lists WIN-025 as 'CONFIRMED.' The V50.6 archive also lists it as CONFIRMED. The generate-html.js does contain the claim 'WIN-025 REMOVED' in the V51.0 changes section, but this appears to be an error in our review, not a fact about the dome model. The dome's live site (john09289.github.io/predictions) does not individually list or de-list WIN-025 — it just counts 67 confirmed WINs. The tracking page has no mention of WIN-025 at all.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify whether the 'REMOVED' status was ever actually present on the dome's site (perhaps briefly between V50.6 and the current V51.0), or if our review incorrectly inferred removal. If the WIN was never removed, our entire rebuttal is based on a false premise and needs a complete rewrite.

holes_found.description: WRONG CONFOUND ATTRIBUTION: Our review claims 'Disturbed geomagnetic days cannot be used to isolate eclipse effects from space weather.' But the April 8, 2024 eclipse occurred during QUIET geomagnetic conditions — Kp ≤ 3, Dst > -25 nT. The 'disturbed-day baseline' caveat in the dome's text applies to WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse), NOT to WIN-025 (2024 eclipse). Our review conflates the two WINs' caveats. The Chernogor & Holub paper specifically notes the quiet conditions, making the 2024 data cleaner than the 2017 data.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the 'disturbed-day' argument entirely for WIN-025. The correct rebuttal for the 2024 eclipse is the standard ionospheric Sq current mechanism, not a space weather confound.

holes_found.description: MISSING SCIENTIFIC REBUTTAL: Our review provides NO scientific argument against WIN-025. It relies entirely on the (apparently false) claim that the author retracted the WIN. The actual strong rebuttal — that eclipse-induced ±5-15 nT magnetic variations have been predicted by standard ionospheric physics (Chapman 1933, Sq current mechanism) for over 90 years — is completely absent. The standard model explanation (eclipse shadow reduces ionospheric E-region conductivity at 90-150 km, disrupting Sq dynamo currents, producing surface magnetic variations) perfectly predicts the magnitude and timing of the observed effects without any dome or aetheric field.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the rebuttal to center on the Sq current mechanism. This is a textbook example of 'Std Model Explains' — the dome model claims credit for an effect that has been understood in standard geophysics since 1933.

stronger_arguments.description: The standard ionospheric Sq current mechanism (Chapman 1933) predicts exactly the kind of magnetic variation observed during eclipses: 5-30 nT surface perturbations with timing that tracks the eclipse shadow geometry, because the shadow physically moves through the ionospheric E-region where the dynamo currents flow. The dome model's claim that the signal 'tracks geometry not solar noon' is actually a prediction of the STANDARD model — the Sq current disruption follows the shadow through the ionosphere. Takeda & Araki (1984) confirmed this with quantitative ionospheric current modeling. Statistical analyses of geomagnetic effects across multiple eclipses (Curto et al., 2006, Advances in Space Research 38:868-876) show consistent 5-30 nT effects with the expected Sq-current signature, with NO residual requiring non-standard physics.
sources: Chapman, S. (1933). The solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism.
Takeda, M. & Araki, T. (1984). Electric currents in the ionosphere during the course of a solar eclipse.
Curto, J.J. et al. (2006). Solar eclipse effects on geomagnetic variations. Adv. Space Res. 38:868-876.
Chernogor & Holub (2024). Geomagnetic effect of the solar eclipse of April 8, 2024. Adv. Space Res. 75(3):3133-3149.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model provides NO derivation of WHY the aetheric field should produce ~10 nT variations. The number is not derived from the ovoid cavity geometry, the aetheric medium properties, or the firmament conductivity. It simply observes that eclipses produce ~10 nT variations (which has been known since the 1930s) and relabels the cause as 'aetheric.' The standard model, by contrast, derives the magnitude from known ionospheric conductivity profiles, Sq current intensity, and the geometry of the shadow's footprint. The dome model's prediction has zero derived quantitative content — it is entirely parasitic on the standard model's successful prediction.
sources: monitor.py eclipse code — the -18.22 nT parameter is hardcoded with no derivation shown

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's claim that the eclipse effect is 'electromagnetic not gravitational' (citing 0.0 μGal from SG gravimeters) is misleading framing. The standard model also predicts the eclipse effect is electromagnetic (ionospheric current disruption), not gravitational. No standard physicist has ever claimed eclipses should produce gravitational anomalies. The SG null result is expected by both models and has zero discriminating power.
sources: Dome model PROS-003 text: 'SG gravimeters confirmed 0.0 uGal — electromagnetic not gravitational'

kernel_of_truth: The Chernogor & Holub (2024) paper is genuine peer-reviewed science. Eclipse-induced geomagnetic variations of ±10 nT at INTERMAGNET stations are real, reproducible, and well-documented. The observation that the magnetic signal tracks the eclipse shadow geometry (rather than local solar time) is also genuine and correctly stated. The dome model correctly identifies a real physical phenomenon. Where the dome model fails is in claiming this phenomenon requires a dome or aetheric field — the standard ionospheric Sq current mechanism has predicted these effects quantitatively since Chapman (1933), and the shadow-tracking geometry is an inherent consequence of the ionospheric conductivity change moving with the shadow.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-025 presents the most serious factual accuracy problem we have found in our review so far. Our rebuttal rests on two claims, both of which appear to be wrong.

First, we state 'The author removed this WIN in version 51.0.' Our own archived raw text from V51.0 contradicts this — line 110 of raw-text/02-wins.txt lists 'WIN-025 — 2024 Eclipse 9-Station Confirmation CONFIRMED (~-10 nT across 9 stations).' The dome's live site counts 67 confirmed WINs. The tracking page does not mention WIN-025. Our generate-html.js does contain a section claiming WIN-025 was marked 'REMOVED' in V51.0, but this appears to be something our review asserted, not something verifiable from the dome's published content. It is possible that a brief intermediate version contained a 'REMOVED' tag that was subsequently reversed, but we have no evidence of this — and we should not make claims we cannot substantiate.

Second, we claim the eclipse data was 'confounded by magnetic storm activity on the observation date.' The April 8, 2024 eclipse occurred during exceptionally quiet geomagnetic conditions — Kp ≤ 3 throughout April 7-9, 2024, with Dst never dropping below -25 nT. The 'disturbed-day baseline' caveat in the dome's own text appears under WIN-010 (the 2017 BOU eclipse at Boulder), not under WIN-025 (the 2024 9-station study). Our review appears to have conflated the two WINs' caveats, applying the 2017 problem to the 2024 data. This is precisely the kind of error that undermines our credibility.

The correct verdict for WIN-025 is almost certainly 'Std Model Explains,' not 'Misleading.' Eclipse-induced geomagnetic variations have been predicted by standard ionospheric physics since Chapman (1933) proposed that the eclipse shadow reduces E-region ionospheric conductivity (90-150 km altitude), disrupting the Sq dynamo current system and producing surface magnetic perturbations of 5-30 nT. This mechanism has been confirmed by Takeda & Araki (1984) through ionospheric current modeling, by statistical analyses across dozens of eclipses (Curto et al., 2006), and by the Chernogor & Holub paper itself, which attributes the observed effects to ionospheric conductivity changes — NOT to any aetheric mechanism. The fact that the signal tracks the eclipse shadow geometry is EXPECTED by the standard model, because the ionospheric conductivity change physically moves with the shadow.

The dome model's contribution here is zero: it provides no derivation of why its ovoid cavity should produce ~10 nT variations, no quantitative prediction from first principles, and no mechanism that distinguishes its prediction from the standard model's well-established one. The monitor.py code hardcodes -18.22 nT for a future 2026 eclipse prediction but shows no derivation. The WIN-025 claim simply cites a standard geophysics paper and relabels the cause as 'aetheric.' Our rebuttal should say this — instead of incorrectly claiming the WIN was removed.
